architecture.summary: Geometric Latent Reasoning (GLR) adds a lightweight transition head to a frozen pretrained language model. During training, the transition head learns to predict continuous embedding-space displacement vectors that approximate discrete token-to-token transitions observed in chain-of-thought reasoning traces. A two-pass training procedure first collects exact discrete transitions, then replaces reasoning-span embeddings with continuous latent states (computed by applying predicted displacements iteratively) and trains the transition head with position-discounted MSE while preserving cross-entropy loss on answer tokens. At inference, GLR executes a fixed number of continuous latent steps in embedding space before resuming standard autoregressive token decoding, effectively shortcutting through early reasoning steps without materializing discrete tokens.
trained_components: latent transition head (linear layer predicting embedding-space displacements)

biblio.title: Geometric Latent Reasoning Induces Shorter Generations in LLMs

claims.statement: On MATH500 with Qwen3-1.7B at a 512-step budget, CoT-SFT solves nearly 0% of problems, while GLR-10 solves over 40%.

claims.metric: median generation length (latent steps + text tokens)
claims.statement: On MATH500 with Qwen3-1.7B, the median correct CoT-SFT generation is approximately 2,000 tokens, whereas GLR-10 and GLR-20 reduce the median to roughly 350 total steps.
On SVAMP with correct solutions, CoT-SFT produces median lengths of roughly 500–700 tokens depending on model size, while GLR solves the same problems in roughly 100 total generated steps.

GLR improves accuracy under constrained generation budgets compared to standard CoT-SFT, but at the largest generation budgets (2048 steps for GSM8K and 4096 steps for MATH500), CoT-SFT recovers and often outperforms GLR in final accuracy.

The effect of latent-step budget is non-monotonic: moderate values yield the strongest accuracy–length tradeoff, whereas large values such as GLR-80 or GLR-100 on Qwen3-1.7B reduce accuracy, suggesting a stability limit for uninterrupted latent reasoning due to accumulated geometric drift.

Ablation on Qwen3-1.7B: GLR model at s_lat=0 (exact discrete updates, no continuous deviation) produces median correct-solution length of approximately 1,000 generated tokens on GSM8K, whereas using learned continuous displacements for s_lat=10 reduces median to under 200 tokens.

claims.dataset: GSM8K, MATH500, SVAMP, MultiArith, AMC23, OlympiadBench
claims.statement: Geometric latent reasoning induces substantially shorter generations without an explicit length objective. The latent-step budget controls an inference-time tradeoff between latent computation budget, output length, and accuracy, exposing a new mechanism for token-efficient reasoning.

GLR adds approximately 1M trainable parameters to Qwen3-0.6B and 4M trainable parameters to Qwen3-1.7B (the lightweight transition head), while freezing all model parameters and the input token-embedding layer.
